nutrient_interactions.effect: Calcium can reduce non-heme iron absorption when taken at the same time, especially at higher calcium doses.
nutrient_interactions.recommendation: Separate calcium and iron supplements when the goal is iron repletion.
references: NIH ODS Iron Fact Sheet for Health Professionals

nutrient_interactions.effect: High-dose zinc can reduce copper absorption and contribute to copper deficiency.
nutrient_interactions.recommendation: Review copper status or copper co-formulation when zinc is high or chronic.
references: NIH ODS Zinc Fact Sheet for Health Professionals

nutrient_interactions.effect: High supplemental calcium and magnesium can compete in absorption contexts, though routine combined use is common.
nutrient_interactions.recommendation: Separate large mineral doses if tolerability or absorption is a concern.
references: NIH ODS Magnesium Fact Sheet for Health Professionals

nutrient_interactions.effect: Vitamin D supports calcium absorption, so high-dose calcium plus vitamin D should be interpreted together for hypercalcemia and UL review.
nutrient_interactions.recommendation: Review the combined calcium and vitamin D exposure rather than each nutrient in isolation.
references: NIH ODS Vitamin D Fact Sheet for Health Professionals
NIH ODS Calcium Fact Sheet for Health Professionals

nutrient_interactions.effect: Vitamin C can increase non-heme iron absorption.
nutrient_interactions.recommendation: Treat this as a beneficial absorption interaction, while still checking iron dose and contraindications.
references: NIH ODS Iron Fact Sheet for Health Professionals

nutrient_interactions.effect: High supplemental folic acid can mask hematologic signs of vitamin B12 deficiency while neurologic injury progresses.
nutrient_interactions.recommendation: Keep B12 status visible when folic acid dose is high.
references: NIH ODS Folate Fact Sheet for Health Professionals
NIH ODS Vitamin B12 Fact Sheet for Health Professionals

medication_interactions.medication_class: Vitamin K antagonists such as warfarin
medication_interactions.effect: Sudden changes in vitamin K intake can destabilise anticoagulation.
medication_interactions.recommendation: Patients using warfarin should keep vitamin K intake consistent and discuss supplementation with the prescriber.
references: NIH ODS Vitamin K Fact Sheet for Health Professionals
Booth SL. Annu Rev Nutr. 2009;29:229-248.

medication_interactions.medication_class: Levothyroxine, tetracycline or quinolone antibiotics, bisphosphonates
medication_interactions.effect: Calcium can bind or reduce absorption of several medicines when taken concurrently.
medication_interactions.recommendation: Separate calcium from affected medicines according to the medicine label or prescriber instructions.
references: NIH ODS Calcium Fact Sheet for Health Professionals

medication_interactions.medication_class: Levothyroxine, tetracycline or quinolone antibiotics, levodopa
medication_interactions.effect: Iron can bind or reduce absorption of several medicines when taken at the same time.
medication_interactions.recommendation: Separate iron from affected medicines and review high-dose iron with a clinician.
references: NIH ODS Iron Fact Sheet for Health Professionals

medication_interactions.effect: Magnesium can chelate these medicines and reduce absorption.
medication_interactions.recommendation: Separate magnesium from affected medicines according to the medicine label or prescriber instructions.
references: NIH ODS Magnesium Fact Sheet for Health Professionals

medication_interactions.effect: Zinc can bind selected medicines and reduce absorption; penicillamine can also alter zinc status.
medication_interactions.recommendation: Separate zinc from affected medicines and review chronic high-dose zinc.
references: NIH ODS Zinc Fact Sheet for Health Professionals

medication_interactions.effect: Potassium supplements can increase hyperkalemia risk when combined with medicines that raise potassium.
medication_interactions.recommendation: Review potassium supplements with the prescriber, especially with kidney disease or potassium-altering medicines.
references: NIH ODS Potassium Fact Sheet for Health Professionals

medication_interactions.effect: Preformed vitamin A plus retinoid exposure can increase toxicity and teratogenicity concern.
medication_interactions.recommendation: Treat high preformed vitamin A as a pregnancy and retinoid-medication review flag.
references: NIH ODS Vitamin A Fact Sheet for Health Professionals

medication_interactions.effect: High-dose vitamin E may increase bleeding risk in anticoagulant or antiplatelet contexts.
medication_interactions.recommendation: Review high-dose vitamin E before surgery or with anticoagulant/antiplatelet therapy.
references: NIH ODS Vitamin E Fact Sheet for Health Professionals

medication_interactions.effect: High iodine intake can alter thyroid management and may interact with thyroid-related medicines.
medication_interactions.recommendation: Review iodine dose, kelp source, and thyroid history with a clinician.
references: NIH ODS Iodine Fact Sheet for Health Professionals

medication_interactions.effect: Chromium may affect glucose management, and some forms may interfere with levothyroxine absorption timing.
medication_interactions.recommendation: Review chromium supplementation with diabetes therapy and separate from levothyroxine when relevant.
references: NIH ODS Chromium Fact Sheet for Health Professionals

medication_interactions.effect: High-dose niacin can add liver, muscle, flushing, and blood-pressure concerns in medication contexts.
medication_interactions.recommendation: Treat high-dose niacin as clinician-review material, especially when doses exceed nutrition-range intake.
references: NIH ODS Niacin Fact Sheet for Health Professionals

medication_interactions.effect: High-dose EPA/DHA can be relevant to bleeding-risk review, although routine food-level intake differs from concentrated supplementation.
medication_interactions.recommendation: Review concentrated EPA/DHA doses with anticoagulant/antiplatelet therapy and surgery plans.
references: NIH ODS Omega-3 Fatty Acids Fact Sheet for Health Professionals

medication_interactions.medication_class: Many prescription medicines including oral contraceptives, cyclosporine, antiretrovirals, digoxin, warfarin, benzodiazepines
medication_interactions.effect: St. John's wort induces CYP enzymes and P-glycoprotein and has documented clinically significant interactions.
medication_interactions.recommendation: Treat as a high-priority medication-review flag for almost any prescription-medication user.
references: NIH NCCIH Clinical Digest: Herb-Drug Interactions

medication_interactions.effect: Ginkgo with warfarin is associated with increased major bleeding risk; efavirenz co-use is not recommended by NCCIH.
medication_interactions.recommendation: Flag for medication review when anticoagulants or antiretrovirals are relevant.
references: NIH NCCIH Clinical Digest: Herb-Drug Interactions

medication_interactions.effect: Green tea at high doses can reduce nadolol exposure, green tea extract can reduce atorvastatin exposure, and concentrated extracts have liver-safety reports.
medication_interactions.recommendation: Separate beverage use from concentrated extract review and check relevant medicines.
references: NIH NCCIH Clinical Digest: Herb-Drug Interactions

medication_interactions.medication_class: Immunosuppression, critical illness, central venous catheter, premature infant care
medication_interactions.effect: Severe and fatal infections have been reported in vulnerable patients given probiotics.
medication_interactions.recommendation: Use probiotic safety review for vulnerable hosts; do not infer safety from CFU count alone.
references: NIH ODS Probiotics Fact Sheet for Health Professionals
NIH NCCIH Dietary and Herbal Supplements
